question: For Clinical Study NCT05091567 titled 'A Phase III, Open-Label Study of Maintenance Lurbinectedin in Combination With Atezolizumab Compared With Atezolizumab in Participants With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
retrieved_chunks: IMforte was a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial done at 96 hospitals and medical centres in 13 countries (Belgium, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy, Mexico, Poland, South Korea, Spain, Taiwan, Türkiye, the UK, and the USA). Eligible patients were aged 18 years or older with treatment-naive ES-SCLC. Patients received four 21-day cycles of induction treatment (atezolizumab, carboplatin, and etoposide). After completing induction treatment, eligible patients without disease progression were randomly assigned (1:1) using permuted blocks (Interactive Voice/Web Response System) to receive maintenance treatment intravenously every 3 weeks with lurbinectedin (3·2 mg/m2; with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor prophylaxis) plus atezolizumab (1200 mg) or atezolizumab (1200 mg). The two primary endpoints were independent review facility-assessed (IRF) progression-free survival and overall survival, measured from randomisation into the maintenance phase. Efficacy endpoints were assessed in the full analysis set, which included all patients who were randomly assigned to maintenance phase treatment, regardless of whether they received their assigned study treatment. Safety was assessed in all patients who received at least one dose of lurbinectedin or atezolizumab, and was analysed according to the treatment received. This study is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT05091567, and is closed for recruitment.

Between Nov 17, 2021, and Jan 11, 2024, 895 patients were screened for enrolment, of whom 660 (74%) were enrolled into the induction phase. Between May 24, 2022, and April 30, 2024, 483 (73%) of 660 patients entered the maintenance phase and were randomly assigned to lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab (n=242) or atezolizumab (n=241). At the data cutoff (July 29, 2024), IRF progression-free survival was longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group (stratified hazard ratio [HR] 0·54 [95% CI 0·43-0·67]; p<0·0001), as was overall survival (stratified HR 0·73 [0·57-0·95]; p=0·017). 92 (38%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and 53 (22%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group had grade 3-4 adverse events. The most common grade 3-4 events in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group were anaemia (20 [8%] of 242 patients), decreased neutrophil count (18 [7%] patients), and decreased platelet count (18 [7%] patients) and the most common events in the atezolizumab group were hyponatremia (five [2%] of 240 patients), dyspnoea (four [2%] patients), and pneumonia (four [2%] patients). Grade 5 adverse events occurred in 12 (5%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and six (3%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group. The incidence of myelosuppressive toxicities (eg, neutropenia and leukopenia) was higher in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group.

IRF progression-free survival and overall survival were longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group for patients with ES-SCLC, albeit with a higher incidence of adverse events. Lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab represents a novel therapeutic option for first-line maintenance treatment in this setting.

At the data cutoff (July 29, 2024), IRF progression-free survival was longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group (stratified hazard ratio [HR] 0·54 [95% CI 0·43-0·67]; p<0·0001), as was overall survival (stratified HR 0·73 [0·57-0·95]; p=0·017). 92 (38%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and 53 (22%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group had grade 3-4 adverse events. The most common grade 3-4 events in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group were anaemia (20 [8%] of 242 patients), decreased neutrophil count (18 [7%] patients), and decreased platelet count (18 [7%] patients) and the most common events in the atezolizumab group were hyponatremia (five [2%] of 240 patients), dyspnoea (four [2%] patients), and pneumonia (four [2%] patients). Grade 5 adverse events occurred in 12 (5%) of 242 patients in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group and six (3%) of 240 patients in the atezolizumab group. The incidence of myelosuppressive toxicities (eg, neutropenia and leukopenia) was higher in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group.

IMforte was a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial done at 96 hospitals and medical centres in 13 countries (Belgium, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy, Mexico, Poland, South Korea, Spain, Taiwan, Türkiye, the UK, and the USA). Eligible patients were aged 18 years or older with treatment-naive ES-SCLC. Patients received four 21-day cycles of induction treatment (atezolizumab, carboplatin, and etoposide). After completing induction treatment, eligible patients without disease progression were randomly assigned (1:1) using permuted blocks (Interactive Voice/Web Response System) to receive maintenance treatment intravenously every 3 weeks with lurbinectedin (3·2 mg/m2; with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor prophylaxis) plus atezolizumab (1200 mg) or atezolizumab (1200 mg). The two primary endpoints were independent review facility-assessed (IRF) progression-free survival and overall survival, measured from randomisation into the maintenance phase. Efficacy endpoints were assessed in the full analysis set, which included all patients who were randomly assigned to maintenance phase treatment, regardless of whether they received their assigned study treatment. Safety was assessed in all patients who received at least one dose of lurbinectedin or atezolizumab, and was analysed according to the treatment received. This study is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT05091567, and is closed for recruitment.

IRF progression-free survival and overall survival were longer in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group than the atezolizumab group for patients with ES-SCLC, albeit with a higher incidence of adverse events. Lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab represents a novel therapeutic option for first-line maintenance treatment in this setting.

draft_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT05091567 include hazard ratios for progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). The stratified hazard ratio for PFS was 0.54 (95% CI 0.43-0.67; p<0.0001), and for OS, it was 0.73 (95% CI 0.57-0.95; p=0.017). Adverse events were more frequent in the lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab group, with 38% experiencing grade 3-4 events compared to 22% in the atezolizumab group. The most common grade 3-4 events in the combination group were anemia (8%), decreased neutrophil count (7%), and decreased platelet count (7%). Grade 5 adverse events occurred in 5% of the combination group and 3% of the atezolizumab group.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
retrieved_chunks:
Participants
Participants were eligible for enrollment if they provided written, informed consent; were
aged ≥18 years; had previously untreated, pathologically confirmed, stage II, IIIA, or
IIIB (N2) NSCLC assessed per the American Joint Committee on Cancer staging system
(8th edition14; details available in the Supplementary Methods and Table S1) considered
resectable after surgical consultation and investigator assessment; had Eastern Cooperative
Oncology Group performance-status score of 0 or 1 (on a 5-point scale, with 0 indicating
no symptoms and higher scores indicating increasing disability15) within 10 days of
randomization; and were able to provide a tumor sample for PD-L1 assessment at a central
laboratory. Full eligibility criteria are available in Section 6 of the protocol, available with
the full text of this article at NEJM.org.
Trial Design and Treatments
In this double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 study, randomization was performed
centrally using an interactive response system. Participants were stratified by stage (II vs
III), PD-L1 tumor proportion score (<50% vs ≥50% based on PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx
[Agilent Technologies]), histology (squamous vs nonsquamous), and geographic region
(east Asia vs not east Asia) and randomized (1:1) to receive pembrolizumab or placebo.
Neoadjuvant therapy comprised four cycles of pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo given
intravenously once every 3 weeks plus cisplatin and gemcitabine (squamous histology)
or cisplatin and pemetrexed (nonsquamous histology); 4 cycles of neoadjuvant therapy
were used in accordance with guideline recommendations for neoadjuvant therapy at the
time of study design in 2017. Surgery was to be performed per local standards no later
than 20 weeks after the first neoadjuvant therapy dose; radiotherapy was administered in
select circumstances. Adjuvant therapy was to be initiated no sooner than 4 weeks and
no later than 12 weeks after surgery and comprised pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo

Participants
Participants were eligible for enrollment if they provided written, informed consent; were
aged ≥18 years; had previously untreated, pathologically confirmed, stage II, IIIA, or
IIIB (N2) NSCLC assessed per the American Joint Committee on Cancer staging system
(8th edition14; details available in the Supplementary Methods and Table S1) considered
resectable after surgical consultation and investigator assessment; had Eastern Cooperative
Oncology Group performance-status score of 0 or 1 (on a 5-point scale, with 0 indicating
no symptoms and higher scores indicating increasing disability15) within 10 days of
randomization; and were able to provide a tumor sample for PD-L1 assessment at a central
laboratory. Full eligibility criteria are available in Section 6 of the protocol, available with
the full text of this article at NEJM.org.
Trial Design and Treatments
In this double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 study, randomization was performed
centrally using an interactive response system. Participants were stratified by stage (II vs
III), PD-L1 tumor proportion score (<50% vs ≥50% based on PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx
[Agilent Technologies]), histology (squamous vs nonsquamous), and geographic region
(east Asia vs not east Asia) and randomized (1:1) to receive pembrolizumab or placebo.
Neoadjuvant therapy comprised four cycles of pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo given
intravenously once every 3 weeks plus cisplatin and gemcitabine (squamous histology)
or cisplatin and pemetrexed (nonsquamous histology); 4 cycles of neoadjuvant therapy
were used in accordance with guideline recommendations for neoadjuvant therapy at the
time of study design in 2017. Surgery was to be performed per local standards no later
than 20 weeks after the first neoadjuvant therapy dose; radiotherapy was administered in
select circumstances. Adjuvant therapy was to be initiated no sooner than 4 weeks and
no later than 12 weeks after surgery and comprised pembrolizumab (200 mg) or placebo

retrieved_chunks: A total of 397 participants were assigned to the pembrolizumab group, and 400 to the placebo group. At the prespecified first interim analysis, the median follow-up was 25.2 months. Event-free survival at 24 months was 62.4% in the pembrolizumab group and 40.6% in the placebo group (hazard ratio for progression, recurrence, or death, 0.58; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.46 to 0.72; P<0.001). The estimated 24-month overall survival was 80.9% in the pembrolizumab group and 77.6% in the placebo group (P = 0.02, which did not meet the significance criterion). A major pathological response occurred in 30.2% of the participants in the pembrolizumab group and in 11.0% of those in the placebo group (difference, 19.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 13.9 to 24.7; P<0.0001; threshold, P = 0.0001), and a pathological complete response occurred in 18.1% and 4.0%, respectively (difference, 14.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 10.1 to 18.7; P<0.0001; threshold, P = 0.0001). Across all treatment phases, 44.9% of the participants in the pembrolizumab group and 37.3% of those in the placebo group had treatment-related adverse events of grade 3 or higher, including 1.0% and 0.8%, respectively, who had grade 5 events.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What secondary endpoints were assessed, including any exploratory ones like quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes
retrieved_chunks:
Wakelee et al. Page 4
given intravenously once every 3 weeks for up to 13 additional cycles. Treatment was
continued until disease progression or recurrence, intolerable toxicity, investigator decision,
withdrawal of consent, or other reasons (summarized in the Supplementary Appendix).
Additional treatment information, including chemotherapy regimen and lymphadenectomy
details and circumstances in which radiotherapy was to be administered, is available in the
Supplementary Appendix.
Assessments and Endpoints
Pathological response following neoadjuvant therapy was assessed by examination of
hematoxylin-and-eosin-stained slides of resected lung tissue and lymph nodes. Definitions
of R0, R1, and R2 resection are outlined in the Supplementary Appendix. Computed
tomography (strongly preferred) or magnetic resonance imaging of the chest and abdomen
was performed during screening, throughout all treatment phases, and during follow-up
according to the schedule outlined in the Supplementary Appendix. While imaging was
performed after neoadjuvant therapy but before surgery, tumors were not formally restaged
before surgery. Participants were contacted for survival every 12 weeks. Adverse events and
laboratory abnormalities were assessed regularly throughout all treatment phases and for 30
days after discontinuation (up to 90 days for serious events in the absence of new anticancer
therapy) and graded according to the National Cancer Institute Common Terminology
Criteria for Adverse Events, version 4.03. Potentially immune-mediated adverse events and
infusion reactions were based on a list of preferred terms prepared by the sponsor and
considered regardless of attribution to treatment by the investigator. EGFR mutation and
ALK translocation status were tested locally per investigator discretion.
Dual primary endpoints were event-free survival (time from randomization to first
occurrence of local progression precluding planned surgery, unresectable tumor, progression
or recurrence per RECIST 1.1 by investigator assessment, or death from any cause)
and overall survival (time from randomization to death from any cause). Key secondary
endpoints included major pathological response (≤10% viable tumor cells in resected
primary tumor and lymph nodes) and pathological complete response (absence of residual
invasive cancer in resected primary tumor and lymph nodes [ypT0/Tis ypN0]) assessed by
blinded, central pathologist examination and safety.
Trial Oversight
A panel of academic advisors and sponsor employees designed the study. An external,
independent data and safety monitoring committee oversees the trial, assessing safety
regularly and efficacy at prespecified interim analyses. The protocol and all amendments
were approved by the appropriate ethics body for each participating center. All authors
vouch for data accuracy and completeness and study conduct in accordance with the
protocol, its amendments, and Good Clinical Practice guidelines. All authors attest they
participated in writing or reviewing and editing the manuscript. A medical writer employed
by the sponsor assisted with manuscript preparation.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': What were the primary endpoint results, including point estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, and whether the primary objective was met
retrieved_chunks:
Wakelee et al. Page 5
Statistical Analysis
Efficacy was assessed in the intention-to-treat population (all randomized participants).
Safety was assessed in the as-treated population (all randomized participants who received
≥1 dose of study treatment). Event-free survival and overall survival were estimated
using the Kaplan-Meier method. The magnitude of the treatment differences (i.e., hazard
ratios and associated 95% confidence intervals [CIs]) was calculated using a stratified
Cox regression model with treatment as a covariate and Efron’s method of handling
ties; between-group differences were assessed using the stratified log-rank test. If the
proportional hazards assumption was not valid, the restricted mean survival time method16
was performed as a sensitivity analysis. Between-group comparisons of the percentage of
participants with major pathological response and the percentage with pathological complete
response were performed using the stratified Miettinen and Nurminen method with strata
weighting by sample size. The randomization stratification factors were applied to all
stratified analyses.
The statistical analysis plan is available in protocol Section 10. The graphical method of
Maurer and Bretz17 was used to strictly control the family-wise type I error rate at one-sided
α=0.025 across the event-free survival, overall survival, major pathological response, and
pathological complete response hypotheses among the interim and final analyses (Fig. S1).
The Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Fleming spending function will be used to control type I error for
event-free survival and overall survival in the interim and final analyses. Power statements
for each hypothesis are in the Supplementary Appendix. The study is considered positive if
at least one of the primary endpoints is significantly improved in the pembrolizumab group.
The data reported herein are from the first interim analysis (data cutoff, July 29, 2022),
which was to be performed ~5 months after the last participant was randomized and after
~326 event-free survival events occurred. On the basis of the observed number of events,
the multiplicity-adjusted one-sided alpha levels at this analysis were 0.00462 for event-free
survival, 0.0001 for major pathological response, and 0.0001 for pathological complete
response.

A total of 397 participants were assigned to the pembrolizumab group, and 400 to the placebo group. At the prespecified first interim analysis, the median follow-up was 25.2 months. Event-free survival at 24 months was 62.4% in the pembrolizumab group and 40.6% in the placebo group (hazard ratio for progression, recurrence, or death, 0.58; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.46 to 0.72; P<0.001). The estimated 24-month overall survival was 80.9% in the pembrolizumab group and 77.6% in the placebo group (P = 0.02, which did not meet the significance criterion). A major pathological response occurred in 30.2% of the participants in the pembrolizumab group and in 11.0% of those in the placebo group (difference, 19.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 13.9 to 24.7; P<0.0001; threshold, P = 0.0001), and a pathological complete response occurred in 18.1% and 4.0%, respectively (difference, 14.2 percentage points; 95% CI, 10.1 to 18.7; P<0.0001; threshold, P = 0.0001). Across all treatment phases, 44.9% of the participants in the pembrolizumab group and 37.3% of those in the placebo group had treatment-related adverse events of grade 3 or higher, including 1.0% and 0.8%, respectively, who had grade 5 events.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03425643 titled 'Efficacy and Safety of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) With Platinum Doublet Chemotherapy as Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy for Participants With Resectable Stage II, IIIA, and Resectable IIIB (T3-4N2) Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (MK-3475-671/KEYNOTE-671)': Summarize the key secondary endpoint findings with hazard ratios, odds ratios, or mean differences, and note any significant adjustments
retrieved_chunks:
Wakelee et al. Page 5
Statistical Analysis
Efficacy was assessed in the intention-to-treat population (all randomized participants).
Safety was assessed in the as-treated population (all randomized participants who received
≥1 dose of study treatment). Event-free survival and overall survival were estimated
using the Kaplan-Meier method. The magnitude of the treatment differences (i.e., hazard
ratios and associated 95% confidence intervals [CIs]) was calculated using a stratified
Cox regression model with treatment as a covariate and Efron’s method of handling
ties; between-group differences were assessed using the stratified log-rank test. If the
proportional hazards assumption was not valid, the restricted mean survival time method16
was performed as a sensitivity analysis. Between-group comparisons of the percentage of
participants with major pathological response and the percentage with pathological complete
response were performed using the stratified Miettinen and Nurminen method with strata
weighting by sample size. The randomization stratification factors were applied to all
stratified analyses.
The statistical analysis plan is available in protocol Section 10. The graphical method of
Maurer and Bretz17 was used to strictly control the family-wise type I error rate at one-sided
α=0.025 across the event-free survival, overall survival, major pathological response, and
pathological complete response hypotheses among the interim and final analyses (Fig. S1).
The Lan-DeMets O’Brien-Fleming spending function will be used to control type I error for
event-free survival and overall survival in the interim and final analyses. Power statements
for each hypothesis are in the Supplementary Appendix. The study is considered positive if
at least one of the primary endpoints is significantly improved in the pembrolizumab group.
The data reported herein are from the first interim analysis (data cutoff, July 29, 2022),
which was to be performed ~5 months after the last participant was randomized and after
~326 event-free survival events occurred. On the basis of the observed number of events,
the multiplicity-adjusted one-sided alpha levels at this analysis were 0.00462 for event-free
survival, 0.0001 for major pathological response, and 0.0001 for pathological complete
response.

excluded from the forest plot because the statistical analysis plan specified that subgroups
with less than 30 participants were to be excluded from the forest plot. PD-L1 denotes
programmed death ligand 1, and TPS tumor proportion score.

draft_answer: The key secondary endpoint findings for Clinical Study NCT03425643 include safety assessments, with the pembrolizumab group showing higher rates of treatment-related adverse events compared to the placebo group. Specifically, 96.7% of participants in the pembrolizumab group experienced any treatment-related adverse event, compared to 95.0% in the placebo group. Grade 3–5 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 44.9% of the pembrolizumab group versus 37.3% in the placebo group. Serious treatment-related adverse events were reported in 17.7% of the pembrolizumab group and 14.3% of the placebo group. Treatment-related adverse events leading to death occurred in 1.0% of the pembrolizumab group and 0.8% of the placebo group. Adverse events leading to discontinuation of all trial treatment were reported in 12.6% of the pembrolizumab group and 5.3% of the placebo group. The study employed stratified Cox regression models and the stratified log-rank test for between-group comparisons, with adjustments for multiple comparisons using the Maurer and Bretz method to control the family-wise type I error rate.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03811002 titled 'Testing the Addition of a New Immunotherapy Drug, Atezolizumab (MPDL3280A), to the Usual Chemoradiation (CRT) Therapy Treatment for Limited Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer (LS-SCLC)': List all exclusion criteria from the study protocol, such as prior treatments, comorbidities, or lab value thresholds that disqualified patients
retrieved_chunks: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03811002; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03811002.

Accrual to oncology clinical trials remains a challenge, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic. For late phase clinical trials funded by the National Cancer Institute, the development of these research protocols is a resource-intensive process; however, mechanisms to optimize patient accrual after trial activation are underdeveloped across the National Clinical Trial Network (NCTN). Low patient accrual can lead to the premature closure of clinical trials and can ultimately delay the availability of new, potentially life-saving therapies in oncology.

NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 has seen robust enrollment, currently 127% of the projected accrual. Importantly, many of the tool kit elements are already being used in ongoing NRG Oncology trials, with 56% of active NRG trials using at least one element of the PI tool kit and all in-development trials offered the resource. This underscores the feasibility and potential benefits of deploying the PI tool kit across all NRG Oncology trials moving forward.

NRG Oncology sought to formally develop a tool kit of resources to use at specific time points during the lifetime of NRG Oncology clinical trials. The tools are clearly described and involve the facilitation of engagement of the study principal investigator with the scientific and patient advocate community during the planning, activation, and accrual periods. Social media tools are also leveraged to enhance such engagement. The principal investigator (PI) tool kit was created in 2019 and thereafter piloted with the NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 phase II or III trial in small-cell lung cancer. The PI tool kit was developed by the NRG Oncology Protocol Operations Management committee and was tested with the NRG/Alliance LU005 randomized trial within the NCTN.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03811002 titled 'Testing the Addition of a New Immunotherapy Drug, Atezolizumab (MPDL3280A), to the Usual Chemoradiation (CRT) Therapy Treatment for Limited Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer (LS-SCLC)': Describe the trial design: Was it randomized, controlled, blinded (single/double), parallel or crossover, and what was the sample size per arm
retrieved_chunks: ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03811002; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03811002.

NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 has seen robust enrollment, currently 127% of the projected accrual. Importantly, many of the tool kit elements are already being used in ongoing NRG Oncology trials, with 56% of active NRG trials using at least one element of the PI tool kit and all in-development trials offered the resource. This underscores the feasibility and potential benefits of deploying the PI tool kit across all NRG Oncology trials moving forward.

NRG Oncology sought to formally develop a tool kit of resources to use at specific time points during the lifetime of NRG Oncology clinical trials. The tools are clearly described and involve the facilitation of engagement of the study principal investigator with the scientific and patient advocate community during the planning, activation, and accrual periods. Social media tools are also leveraged to enhance such engagement. The principal investigator (PI) tool kit was created in 2019 and thereafter piloted with the NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 phase II or III trial in small-cell lung cancer. The PI tool kit was developed by the NRG Oncology Protocol Operations Management committee and was tested with the NRG/Alliance LU005 randomized trial within the NCTN.

The purpose of this study is to formally create an easily implemented tool kit of resources for investigators of oncology clinical trials within the NCTN, specifically the NRG Oncology cooperative group, in order to optimize patient accrual.

While clinical trial accrual can be challenging, the PI tool kit has been shown to augment accrual in a low-cost and easily implementable fashion. It could be widely and consistently deployed across the NCTN to improve accrual in oncology clinical trials.

retrieved_chunks: NRG Oncology sought to formally develop a tool kit of resources to use at specific time points during the lifetime of NRG Oncology clinical trials. The tools are clearly described and involve the facilitation of engagement of the study principal investigator with the scientific and patient advocate community during the planning, activation, and accrual periods. Social media tools are also leveraged to enhance such engagement. The principal investigator (PI) tool kit was created in 2019 and thereafter piloted with the NRG Oncology/Alliance NRG-LU005 phase II or III trial in small-cell lung cancer. The PI tool kit was developed by the NRG Oncology Protocol Operations Management committee and was tested with the NRG/Alliance LU005 randomized trial within the NCTN.

ClinicalTrials.gov NCT03811002; https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03811002.

While clinical trial accrual can be challenging, the PI tool kit has been shown to augment accrual in a low-cost and easily implementable fashion. It could be widely and consistently deployed across the NCTN to improve accrual in oncology clinical trials.

The purpose of this study is to formally create an easily implemented tool kit of resources for investigators of oncology clinical trials within the NCTN, specifically the NRG Oncology cooperative group, in order to optimize patient accrual.

draft_answer: Participants in the clinical trial NCT04676412 were required to have PD-L1-positive, treatment-naïve non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Key inclusion criteria included being PD-L1-positive, having locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC, and meeting specific health and biomarker requirements. Exclusion criteria included active autoimmune diseases requiring systemic treatment, history of non-infectious pneumonitis, and certain cardiovascular conditions. The exact age range is not specified in the provided contexts.
draft_citations: Patients were ineligible if they had clinically important hemoptysis (defined as 0.5 teaspoon of bright red blood) or tumor bleeding within 2 weeks before study treatment; either known central nervous system metastases or carcinomatous meningitis or both, unless previously treated and radiologically stable (i.e., no evidence of progression for 4 weeks per repeat imaging performed during screening), clinically stable, and with no steroid treatment required within 14 days before study treatment; active autoimmune disease that required systemic treatment in the previous 2 years; history of non-infectious pneumonitis that required steroids or current pneumonitis/interstitial lung disease; diagnosis of immunodeficiency or immunosuppressive therapy within 7 days before study treatment; history of a gastrointestinal condition/procedure that could affect absorption of oral lenvatinib; or clinically relevant cardiovascular impairment (e.g., congestive heart failure classified as greater than New York Heart Association class II, unstable angina, myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or cardiac arrhythmia associated with hemodynamic instability) within 12 months before study treatment.

Archival tumor tissue samples or newly obtained core or excisional biopsy samples of a tumor lesion not previously irradiated were centrally evaluated for PD-L1 expression during screening using PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx (Agilent Technologies, Carpinteria, CA).
draft_missing_info: Age range is not explicitly mentioned in the provided contexts.

draft_answer: Exclusion criteria for the study included: history of gastrointestinal conditions affecting lenvatinib absorption; clinically relevant cardiovascular impairment (e.g., congestive heart failure, unstable angina, myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or arrhythmia with hemodynamic instability) within 12 months; prior anti-PD-1/anti-PD-L1 therapy or lenvatinib treatment; recent radiotherapy (within 14 days or >30 Gy within 6 months); radiographic evidence of major blood vessel invasion or intratumoral cavitation; clinically important hemoptysis (defined as 0.5 teaspoon of bright red blood); central nervous system metastases or carcinomatous meningitis unless previously treated and stable; active autoimmune disease requiring systemic treatment; history of non-infectious pneumonitis requiring steroids; immunodeficiency or immunosuppressive therapy within 7 days of treatment; and brain metastases or liver metastases not meeting specific stability criteria.
draft_citations: history of a gastrointestinal condition/procedure that could affect absorption of orallenvatinib; or clinically relevant cardiovascular impairment (e.g., congestive heart failure classified as greater than New York Heart Association class II, unstable angina, myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or cardiac arrhythmia associated with hemodynamic instability) within 12 months before study treatment.

Additional exclusion criteria included prior anti‒PD-1/anti‒PD-L1/anti‒PD-L2 therapy or any treatment directed at another stimulatory or co-inhibitory T-cell receptor, prior lenvatinib treatment as monotherapy or in combination with anti‒PD-1 agents, or any radiotherapy within 14 days or lung radiotherapy of more than 30 Gy within 6 months before initiation of study treatment.

Patients were ineligible if there was radiographic evidence of invasion or infiltration of a major blood vessel. Despite this, fatal bleeding events occurred in a few patients who were retrospectively found to have major blood vessel encasement at enrollment, and thus the protocol was amended to specify exclusion of encasement of a major blood vessel or intratumoral cavitation.

Patients were ineligible if they had clinically important hemoptysis (defined as 0.5 teaspoon of bright red blood) or tumor bleeding within 2 weeks before study treatment; either known central nervous system metastases or carcinomatous meningitis or both, unless previously treated and radio-logically stable (i.e., no evidence of progression for 4 weeks per repeat imaging during screening), clinically stable, and with no steroid treatment required within 14 days before study treatment; active autoimmune disease that required systemic treatment in the previous 2 years; history of non-infectious pneumonitis that required steroids or current pneumonitis/interstitial lung disease; diagnosis of immunodeficiency or immunosuppressive therapy within 7 days before study treatment.
draft_missing_info: No significant gaps in the provided contexts regarding exclusion criteria.

draft_answer: The primary endpoints of the study were Overall Survival (OS) and Progression-Free Survival (PFS). OS was measured as the time from randomization to death from any cause, and PFS was measured as the time from randomization to the first documented assessment of progressive disease (PD). The predefined success thresholds were a hazard ratio (HR) less than 0.9 for OS and an HR less than 1.0 for PFS.
draft_citations: The study had two primary end points: overall survival (OS), defined as time from randomization to death because of any cause, and progression-free survival (PFS), defined as time from randomization to the first documented assessment of PD (per RECIST version 1.1)
If the OS HR was less than 0.9, then the futility criterion was not met.
If the PFS HR was less than 1, representing no PFS harm, then the futility criterion was not met.
draft_missing_info: No missing information.

draft_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study included Objective Response Rate (ORR) and safety. An exploratory endpoint was the duration of response, defined as the time from the earliest documented response to the first occurrence of progressive disease or death. Quality-of-life scores and biomarker changes were not mentioned as endpoints in the provided contexts.
draft_citations: Secondary end points were ORR, defined as a confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR, and safety. Duration of response, defined as time from earliest documented response to the first of either PD or death because of any cause, was an exploratory end point.
Secondary end points were ORR, defined as a confirmed complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) per RECIST version 1.1 by BICR, and safety.
draft_missing_info: No information was provided on quality-of-life scores or biomarker changes as endpoints.

metadatas.section_title: 6.2 (0.07‒23.4) months in the lenvatinib plus pem-

draft_answer: The primary endpoints for the China Extension Study were overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS). The results suggested worse OS outcomes with lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab compared to placebo plus pembrolizumab, with a wide confidence interval likely due to the smaller sample size and low proportion of events. The primary objective was not met as the study did not demonstrate significant efficacy in OS, PFS, or ORR.
draft_citations: Chunk 3: Nevertheless, it was recently reported that at the final study analysis, both LEAP-006 and LEAP-008 did not meet their primary and secondary efficacy end points, including OS, PFS, and ORR. The full publication of data from these studies could provide insight into which patient population may have benefited the most from this treatment combination. In conclusion, although there was evidence of clinical benefit based on PFS and ORR findings with lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab in this randomized, double-blind,

Chunk 3: Similar to the global study, results from the China study suggested worse OS outcomes with lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab versus placebo plus pembrolizumab, although the CI was wide, likely owing to the smaller sample size and low proportion of events.
draft_missing_info: Specific point estimates, confidence intervals, and p-values for the primary endpoints in the China Extension Study were not explicitly provided in the retrieved contexts.

draft_answer: The secondary endpoint findings included a hazard ratio (HR) for progression-free survival (PFS) of 0.78 (95% CI, 0.64-0.95) with a nominal p-value of 0.00624. The HR for overall survival (OS) was 1.10 (95% CI not provided in the chunk). The study also evaluated objective response rate (ORR) as a secondary endpoint, but it was only formally tested at the first interim analysis. The futility analysis for OS had a boundary of HR ≤ 0.9, and for PFS, the boundary was HR < 1.0. The alpha initially assigned to ORR (one-sided alpha of 0.001) was reallocated to the primary endpoints of PFS and OS.

draft_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in the lenvatinib + pembrolizumab arm were hypertension (17.2%), proteinuria (6.1%), and diarrhea (3.2%). In the placebo + pembrolizumab arm, the most common were hypertension (2.6%), proteinuria (1.0%), and diarrhea (0.6%). Serious events leading to discontinuation occurred in 29.1% of the lenvatinib + pembrolizumab group and 11.2% of the placebo + pembrolizumab group.

metadatas.section_title: 6.2 (0.07‒23.4) months in the lenvatinib plus pem-

draft_answer: The primary analysis used Kaplan-Meier estimates for OS and PFS, stratified log-rank tests for between-group differences, and stratified Cox proportional hazard models with Efron’s method for handling ties. The intention-to-treat population was analyzed for efficacy, while the as-treated population was used for safety. Multiplicity corrections included reallocating alpha levels to primary endpoints (OS and PFS) and prespecified futility boundaries.
draft_citations: Efficacy analyses were performed in all randomized patients according to assigned treatment (intention-to-treat population). Safety analyses were performed in all randomized patients who received at least one dose of the study treatment according to treatment received (as-treated population). OS and PFS were estimated using the nonparametric Kaplan-Meier method. Between-group treatment differences in OS and PFS were assessed using the stratified log-rank test; all p-values are one-sided. The magnitude of the treatment difference was calculated using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model with Efron’s method of tie handling.

The protocol was amended to reallocate the alpha initially assigned to the secondary end point of ORR [...] to the primary end points of PFS and OS, with analysis planned after approximately 416 PFS events had occurred
For the futility analysis, OS was evaluated first using a p-value futility boundary of 0.4960. If the p-value was less than 0.4960, the futility criterion was not met.
draft_missing_info: No significant gaps in the provided contexts regarding the statistical methods used for the primary analysis.

draft_answer: The inclusion criteria for participants in the clinical trial NCT05089734 included patients with stage IV disease at study entry and an age range of 31 to 84 years. Specific biomarkers required for inclusion were not explicitly mentioned in the provided contexts.
draft_citations: Between November 17, 2021, and May 30, 2023, 603 patients with stage IV disease at study entry from 177 sites in 20 countries were randomly assigned to receive SG (n = 299) or docetaxel (n = 304; Fig 1).
Patients in the SG and docetaxel groups had a median age of 65 years (range, 31-84) and 64 years (range, 32-83), respectively.
draft_missing_info: Biomarker requirements for inclusion were not specified in the provided contexts.

draft_answer: The secondary endpoints included progression-free survival (PFS), overall response rate (ORR), duration of response (DoR), disease control rate (DCR), and time to deterioration (TTD) in the NSCLC-SAQ. PFS and TTD were analyzed using the Kaplan-Meier method with stratified log-rank tests, and response rates were compared using the Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel method. Safety assessments included treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs), with 99.7% of patients in the SG group and 97.9% in the docetaxel group experiencing TEAEs. Significant adjustments included a Lan-DeMets alpha spending function approximating an O'Brien-Fleming approach for interim analysis.
draft_citations: Secondary efficacy end points were investigator-assessed progression-free survival (PFS), ORR (percentage of patients with confirmed CR or PR), DoR, disease control rate (DCR; percentage of patients with confirmed SD, CR, or PR) as determined by RECIST version 1.1, and safety.

OS, PFS, and TTD were analyzed using the Kaplan-Meier method, with 95% CIs determined according to the Brookmeyer-Crowley method. Between-treatment group differences were assessed by stratified log-rank test. HRs and associated 95% CIs were estimated by a stratified Cox proportional hazards model. Response rates were compared between groups by the stratified Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel method.

TEAEs were summarized in each group using descriptive statistics for treatment-emergent AEs (TEAEs; defined as any AEs that began or worsened on/after study drug administration through 30 days after last dose of study drug) unless otherwise specified.
draft_missing_info: Specific hazard ratios or odds ratios for secondary endpoints not explicitly provided in the retrieved contexts.

draft_answer: The most common adverse events (grade 3+) in the SG arm were fatigue (12.5%), diarrhea (10.5%), and neutropenia (7.8%), while in the docetaxel arm, the most common were fatigue (9.7%), neutropenia (9.4%), and dyspnea (4.5%). Serious events leading to discontinuation included febrile neutropenia, sepsis, and others, affecting 9.8% in SG and 16.7% in docetaxel.
draft_citations: Chunk 2: The proportion of patients with TEAEs leading to study treatment discontinuation was lower with SG (n = 29, 9.8%) than docetaxel (n = 48, 16.7%).

draft_answer: The primary analysis used Kaplan-Meier method with Brookmeyer-Crowley 95% CIs, stratified log-rank test, and stratified Cox proportional hazards model for HRs. Multiplicity was addressed using a Lan-DeMets alpha spending function approximating an O’Brien-Fleming approach. ITT population was used for efficacy assessments.
draft_citations: OS, PFS, and TTD were analyzed using the Kaplan-Meier method, with 95% CIs determined according to the Brookmeyer-Crowley method. Between-treatment group differences were assessed by stratified log-rank test. HRs and associated 95% CIs were estimated by a stratified Cox proportional hazards model.
A Lan-DeMets alpha spending function that approximates an O’Brien-Fleming approach was used to account for multiplicity introduced by including an interim analysis for OS.
Efficacy end points were evaluated in the ITT population.
draft_missing_info: No significant gaps in the provided contexts regarding statistical methods.

draft_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed included progression-free survival (PFS), overall survival (OS), confirmed objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), PFS rate at 6 and 12 months, and OS rate at 12 and 24 months. Exploratory endpoints included biomarker analyses, such as the TIGIT pathway, and adverse events (AEs) evaluation. Quality-of-life scores were not mentioned in the provided contexts.
draft_citations: Secondary end points were PFS and OS in all randomly assigned patients, irrespective of brain metastases status at baseline (full analysis set [FAS]); confirmed objective response rate (ORR), defined as the proportion of patients with a complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) on two consecutive occasions ≥4 weeks apart; duration of response (DOR) for patients with confirmed ORR; PFS rate at 6 and 12 months; and OS rate at 12 and 24 months. One efficacy interim and one final analysis of OS was planned.

Adverse events (AEs) were evaluated in all randomly assigned patients who received at least one dose of study treatment, with severity assessed according to National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events version 5.0.

Pre- and on-treatment samples of tumor tissue, serum, plasma, and blood were collected as part of the exploratory biomarker objective to identify potential biomarkers of treatment response, including the TIGIT pathway, prognosis, acquired resistance, pharmacodynamics, and safety.
draft_missing_info: Information on quality-of-life scores was not provided in the retrieved contexts.

draft_answer: The secondary endpoints included PFS and OS in the full analysis set (FAS), confirmed objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), PFS rates at 6 and 12 months, and OS rates at 12 and 24 months. The study did not provide specific hazard ratios or odds ratios for these secondary endpoints but outlined that the primary analysis of PFS in the PAS was planned when approximately 300 PFS events were observed, with a target PFS hazard ratio (HR) of 0.56 at a two-sided significance level of 0.001. The final analysis of OS in the PAS was planned when approximately 288 deaths were observed, with a target OS HR of 0.70 at a two-sided significance level of 0.049. The study used a stratified log-rank test and stratified Cox proportional hazards model for comparisons, with a sample size of approximately 400 patients targeted for the PAS.
draft_citations: Chunk 2: The primary efficacy end points were PFS and OS in all randomly assigned patients without history/presence of brain metastases at baseline (primary analysis set [PAS]). Secondary end points were PFS and OS in all randomly assigned patients, irrespective of brain metastases status at baseline (full analysis set [FAS]); confirmed objective response rate (ORR), defined as the proportion of patients with a complete response (CR) or partial response (PR) on two consecutive occasions ≥4 weeks apart; duration of response (DOR) for patients with confirmed ORR; PFS rate at 6 and 12 months; and OS rate at 12 and 24 months.

Chunk 2: The final analysis of the primary end point, OS in the PAS, was planned for when approximately 288 deaths had been observed in the PAS. This would provide 85% power to detect a target OS HR of 0.70 at a two-sided significance level of 0.049, assuming a median OS of 12.3 months in the placebo plus atezolizumab and CE arm and 17.6 months in the tiragolumab plus atezolizumab and CE arm.
Chunk 2: The stratified log-rank test was used to compare PFS and OS between treatment arms; the HR for PFS and OS was estimated using a stratified Cox proportional hazards model.
draft_missing_info: The study does not provide specific hazard ratios or odds ratios for the secondary endpoints beyond the primary analysis.

draft_answer: The primary analysis used the stratified log-rank test to compare PFS and OS between treatment arms. The Cox proportional hazards model was used to estimate hazard ratios. Kaplan-Meier methodology was employed to estimate median PFS and OS, with the Brookmeyer-Crowley method used to construct 95% confidence intervals. The study used a hierarchical testing approach, allocating a two-sided alpha of 0.001 and 0.049 to PFS and OS in the PAS, respectively, to control the overall type I error rate at 0.05. The sample size was targeted at approximately 400 patients in the PAS, assuming a 15% prevalence of brain metastases at baseline, and approximately 470 patients were randomly assigned in the study.
draft_citations: Survival analyses were performed in the PAS and FAS, with patients grouped according to assigned treatment. [...] The stratified log-rank test was used to compare PFS and OS between treatment arms; the HR for PFS and OS was estimated using a stratified Cox proportional hazards model. Kaplan-Meier methodology was used to estimate median PFS and OS, and the Brookmeyer-Crowley method was used to construct 95% CIs.
To control the overall type 1 error rate at 0.05 (two-sided), endpoints were tested hierarchically. A two-sided α of .001 and .049 was allocated to PFS and OS in the PAS, respectively.

A sample size of approximately 400 patients was targeted for the PAS, assuming a 15% prevalence of presence or history of brain metastases at baseline. It was estimated that approximately 470 patients would be randomly assigned within the study.
draft_missing_info: No significant gaps in the provided contexts regarding the statistical methods used for the primary analysis.

The KEYVIBE program consisting of nine trials will evaluate the safety and efficacy of the anti-TIGIT antibody vibostolimab across several tumor types.Trial registration: KEYVIBE-001 (NCT02964013); KEYVIBE-002 (NCT04725188); KEYVIBE-003 (NCT04738487); KEYVIBE-004 (NCT05005442); KEYVIBE-005 (NCT05007106); KEYVIBE-006 (NCT05298423); KEYVIBE-007 (NCT05226598); KEYVIBE-008 (NCT05224141); and KEYVIBE-010 (NCT05665595) (ClinicalTrials.gov).

LUNG CANCER—NON-SMALL CELL METASTATIC
TPS9160 Poster Session

pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy as first-line therapy for metastatic non–small cell
lungcancer(NSCLC):KeyVibe-007.
Marina Chiara Garassino, Enriqueta Felip, Mark M. Awad, Wei Wang, Sung Jin Kim,
Maria Catherine Pietanza, Delvys Rodriguez-Abreu; UniversityofChicagoMedicine&BiologicalSci-
ences,KnappCenterforBiomedicalDiscovery(KCBD),Chicago,IL;Valld’HebronUniversityHospital
and Institute of Oncology (VHIO), Medical Oncology Department, Barcelona, Spain; Lowe Center for

ComplejoHospitalarioUniversitarioInsularMaterno-InfantildeGranCanaria,UniversidaddeLasPal-

Background:Pembrolizumab(pembro)pluschemotherapyisastandardfirst-linetreatmentforpatients
withmetastaticNSCLCwithoutEGFRorALKgenomicalterationsregardlessofPD-L1expression.The
combination of pembro and vibostolimab, a humanized monoclonal antibody that blocks the interac-
tion between the T-cell immunoreceptor with immunoglobulin and ITIM domains (TIGIT) and its li-
gands CD112 and CD155, showed antitumor activity and manageable safety in patients with
advanced or metastatic NSCLC in the phase 1 MK-7684-001 study. The phase 3 KeyVibe-007
(NCT05226598)studyisevaluatingtheefficacyandsafetyofpembroandvibostolimab,coformulated
asMK-7684A,incombinationwithchemotherapyversuspembropluschemotherapyasfirst-linether-
apyformetastaticNSCLC.Methods:hisrandomized,double-blindstudyisenrollingadultswithhisto-
logically or cytologically confirmed,previously untreated, stageIV squamous or nonsquamous NSCLC
without EGFR mutations or ALK/ROS1 gene rearrangements.

Vibostolimab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody, blocks the interaction between TIGIT and its ligands, preventing the immunosuppressive effects of TIGIT. The addition of vibostolimab to the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has shown promising antitumor activity, warranting further exploration of vibostolimab as a potential therapeutic option. The KEYVIBE program consists of nine trials that will evaluate the safety and efficacy of vibostolimab monotherapy and vibostolimab-based combination therapy in advanced solid tumors and hematological malignancies. These studies will also evaluate coformulated immunotherapy addressing issues that occur with the sequential administration of immunotherapy. The KEYVIBE program will provide further insight into the clinical utility of vibostolimab-based therapy across multiple indications and various stages of disease.

Vibostolimab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody, blocks the interaction between TIGIT and its ligands, preventing the immunosuppressive effects of TIGIT. The addition of vibostolimab to the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has shown promising antitumor activity, warranting further exploration of vibostolimab as a potential therapeutic option. The KEYVIBE program consists of nine trials that will evaluate the safety and efficacy of vibostolimab monotherapy and vibostolimab-based combination therapy in advanced solid tumors and hematological malignancies. These studies will also evaluate coformulated immunotherapy addressing issues that occur with the sequential administration of immunotherapy. The KEYVIBE program will provide further insight into the clinical utility of vibostolimab-based therapy across multiple indications and various stages of disease.

draft_answer: The secondary endpoints assessed in the study include overall response rate (ORR) and duration of response per RECIST v1.1 by blinded independent central review (BICR). Additionally, changes from baseline and time to true deterioration in patient-reported outcomes (PROs) were evaluated using validated instruments such as the EORTC Quality of Life Questionnaire Lung Cancer 13 and NSCLC Symptom Assessment Questionnaire. Safety was also assessed, with adverse events graded according to the National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events v5.0.
draft_citations: Secondary endpoints are ORR and duration of response per RECIST v1.1 by BICR, change from baseline and time to true deterioration in patient-reported outcomes (PROs), and safety.
PROs are assessed using validated instruments including the EORTC Quality of Life Questionnaire Lung Cancer 13 and NSCLC Symptom Assessment Questionnaire.

Vibostolimab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody, blocks the interaction between TIGIT and its ligands, preventing the immunosuppressive effects of TIGIT. The addition of vibostolimab to the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has shown promising antitumor activity, warranting further exploration of vibostolimab as a potential therapeutic option. The KEYVIBE program consists of nine trials that will evaluate the safety and efficacy of vibostolimab monotherapy and vibostolimab-based combination therapy in advanced solid tumors and hematological malignancies. These studies will also evaluate coformulated immunotherapy addressing issues that occur with the sequential administration of immunotherapy. The KEYVIBE program will provide further insight into the clinical utility of vibostolimab-based therapy across multiple indications and various stages of disease.

Vibostolimab, a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody, blocks the interaction between TIGIT and its ligands, preventing the immunosuppressive effects of TIGIT. The addition of vibostolimab to the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has shown promising antitumor activity, warranting further exploration of vibostolimab as a potential therapeutic option. The KEYVIBE program consists of nine trials that will evaluate the safety and efficacy of vibostolimab monotherapy and vibostolimab-based combination therapy in advanced solid tumors and hematological malignancies. These studies will also evaluate coformulated immunotherapy addressing issues that occur with the sequential administration of immunotherapy. The KEYVIBE program will provide further insight into the clinical utility of vibostolimab-based therapy across multiple indications and various stages of disease.

question: For Clinical Study NCT03447769 titled 'Brief Title: Study of Efficacy and Safety of Canakinumab as Adjuvant Therapy in Adult Subjects With Stages AJCC/UICC v. 8 II-IIIA and IIIB (T>5cm N2) Completely Resected Non-small Cell Lung Cancer Acronym: CANOPY-A': What were the exact inclusion criteria for participants in this clinical trial, including age range, disease stage, and required biomarkers
retrieved_chunks:
significant. Other secondary end points included DFS and OS by biomarker subgroups, lung
cancer–specific survival (LCSS), safety, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity. Details of LCSS
methods and a supplementary analysis to estimate the treatment effects on the basis of DFS had
the COVID-19 pandemic not occurred are provided in Appendix 1 (online only).
Patient Population
Adult patients with completely resected, AJCC/UICC version 8 stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-
positive disease) NSCLC were recruited. Patients had received and recovered from at least two
cycles of cisplatin-based therapy within 60 days of surgery (except for patients with stage IIA
disease with no nodal involvement, unless recommended by the treating physician) and radiation
therapy if indicated per local practice. Eligible patients had adequate organ function and an
Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 0 or 1. Patients were eligible
regardless of driver mutation status. Patients with unresectable or metastatic disease, malignant
disease other than NSCLC, or lung interstitial disease grade ≥2, and those who had received
neoadjuvant therapy, were ineligible.
Assessments
Imaging (computed tomography [CT] and/or magnetic resonance imaging) was performed at
screening/baseline, once every 12 weeks during year 1, once every 26 weeks during years 2 and 3,
and annually during years 4 and 5. First disease recurrence was detected by CT scan and a biopsy
was performed in cases of nonconclusive radiologic evidence. Patients who discontinued treatment
were followed up once every 12 weeks for survival until the final OS analysis or until death, loss to
follow-up, or consent withdrawal for survival follow-up.
Safety was monitored. All adverse events (AEs) were reported for at least 130 days after the last
administration of canakinumab and graded according to the Common Terminology Criteria for AEs
version 5.
Biomarker analyses were conducted using tumor tissue and blood samples. Collection of archival,
resected tumor samples during screening was mandatory. Additional tumor samples (core,
excisional, and incisional biopsies) were collected at the time of disease recurrence. Correlative
analyses reported focus on the baseline tissue collection time point only, because of availability
from most patients, and depended on testing priority, tissue quality, and local regulations.
Collection of blood samples for circulating biomarker analyses (eg, IL-6 and CRP) was mandatory
at baseline, on treatment, at post-treatment follow-up, and at disease progression. Detailed
biomarker assessments are described in Appendix 1.
Statistical Analysis
The full analysis set comprised all patients assigned to study treatment by random assignment.
DFS of canakinumab versus placebo arms was analyzed using a stratified log-rank test at an overall
one-sided 2.5% significance level. This study had a two-look (Lan-DeMets) group sequential design
with an O'Brien-Fleming type α-spending function. A user-defined γ function (with γ = –3) was

Patients enrolled between April 2018 and December 2021 and were randomly assigned 1:1
(permuted block random assignment) to receive subcutaneous canakinumab 200 mg or placebo
once every 3 weeks for 18 cycles (Figs 1 and 2). Patients were stratified by stage (IIA v IIB v IIIA v
IIIB with T >5 cm, N2-positive disease), as defined according to the American Joint Committee on
Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control (AJCC/UICC) version 8 stage; histology (squamous v
nonsquamous); and geographical region (western Europe and North America v eastern Asia v rest
of the world).

In light of the COVID-19 pandemic and its potential impacts on treatment effects, a supplementary
analysis censored patients who died or discontinued treatment due to COVID-19, or who started
medication for suspected/confirmed COVID-19. On the basis of this analysis, there was no
treatment effect (HR, 0.96; 95% CI, 0.79 to 1.16).
The key secondary end point, OS, was not formally tested as DFS was not statistically significant
(OS HR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.48 to 1.08; Fig 4A). Only 100 events were reported: 44 (6.3%) in the
canakinumab arm and 56 (8.1%) in the placebo arm. LCSS (defined as the time from the date of
random assignment to the date of death due to lung cancer) was similar between arms (HR, 0.90;
95% CI, 0.53 to 1.52; Fig 4B). Overall, 60 LCSS events were observed, including 30 events in the
canakinumab arm and 30 events in the placebo arm. Fourteen patients on canakinumab and 26
patients on placebo died due to other reasons and were censored for the LCSS analysis.

effectively inhibited IL-1β signaling in CANOPY-A. Although individual doses in CANOPY-A were
lower than in CANTOS, on the basis of considerably more frequent dosing, dose density was
greater. Although CANTOS enrolled patients who were required to have a CRP level above 2 mg/L,
no threshold was set for patients enrolled in CANOPY-A. As patients with lung cancer have been
reported to exhibit increased levels of inflammation,23,24 it was expected that the CANOPY-A study
population would be enriched for elevated CRP without the need to set a threshold. Additionally,
not setting a limit on baseline CRP level ensured further investigation into the relationship
between CRP level and clinical outcomes with canakinumab would be possible, as well as that
patients potentially capable of benefiting from IL-1β inhibition were not excluded. The baseline
CRP levels in CANOPY-A were lower than anticipated and reflect a relatively low inflammatory
status of patients with NSCLC after surgical resection and chemotherapy. There was no DFS benefit
with canakinumab in CANOPY-A in the subgroup of patients who had a baseline CRP level above 2
mg/L. In CANOPY-1, there was a trend for OS benefit with canakinumab in the lowest quartile of
CRP levels.18 Taken together, baseline and decreases in CRP levels appear not to be relevant
biomarkers for predicting or determining response to canakinumab for lung cancer relapse in the
covered range of baseline CRP levels.25 Further molecular analyses in CANOPY-A are currently
ongoing and could help identify subgroups of patients who may benefit from canakinumab in the
adjuvant setting.
The end points also differed between CANTOS and CANOPY-A. Lung cancer incidence was not a
prespecified efficacy end point in CANTOS, and occurrence was derived from AE data. Thus, the
strength of these data might have been overestimated. As the most common cause of cancer
death is lung cancer, it would likely have been the only disease with the potential to show a
reduction in cancer deaths. However, the analyses did not adjust for multiple hypothesis testing.
The fact that one type (lung cancer) of one class of AEs (cancer) was analyzed without adjusting for
multiplicity could explain why the exploratory evidence from CANTOS was not confirmed in this
study.
In a retrospective analysis of the CANTOS trial, some patients diagnosed with lung cancer during
treatment with canakinumab were determined to be positive for ctDNA at baseline and, therefore,
likely had undiagnosed lung cancer at the start of treatment. This led to the assumption that
canakinumab could prevent progression of micrometastatic disease, and avoid progression to
clinically detectable disease and noticeable symptoms. It is important to note that the limited
ctDNA data set prevented the possibility of making a firm conclusion on whether reduced lung
cancer incidence and mortality after canakinumab treatment were due to reduced disease
progression or prevention of lung cancer development over the course of the CANTOS trial
(including patients with ctDNA-negative results at baseline). If canakinumab only has an impact on
the prevention of lung cancer development, but not on disease progression, this may explain the
negative outcome of the CANOPY-A study. Furthermore, in the CANOPY-1 trial, symptoms of lung
cancer were reduced in the canakinumab arm.

Additional CANOPY-A study limitations include the long time
from diagnosis to random assignment, based, in part, on the need for chemotherapy in most
patients and radiation therapy in some; short time from random assignment to data cutoff; and
lack of pathologic confirmation of recurrent disease.
Although not formally tested, OS was numerically greater in the canakinumab arm compared with
the placebo arm. Only 100 deaths were reported, and the separation of the Kaplan-Meier curves
occurred after completion of therapy. Furthermore, LCSS rates were similar between the two
treatment arms, with relatively more patient deaths, not from lung cancer, in the placebo arm,
including cardiovascular disease–related fatalities.
Different factors may underpin the disparate results from CANTOS and CANOPY-A, such as the
differences in the populations recruited: patients had cardiovascular disease and myocardial
infarction versus patients with NSCLC, respectively. CRP level was higher in patients diagnosed with
lung cancer in CANTOS than in patients in CANOPY-A (6.0 mg/L v 1.8 mg/L, respectively).16
Nevertheless, the effect of canakinumab on CRP level in CANOPY-A was similar to that observed in
the CANTOS trial, with a significant CRP reduction of up to 41%,16 suggesting that canakinumab

At data cutoff, 337 (48.6%) and 349 (50.7%) patients had completed treatment in the canakinumab
and placebo arms, respectively. Treatment was discontinued before completion in 207 (29.9%) and
203 (29.5%) patients in the canakinumab and placebo arms, respectively (Appendix Table A1). The
main reasons for discontinuation were progressive disease (18.9% and 19.2% in the canakinumab
and placebo arms, respectively), AEs (4.5% and 4.2% in the canakinumab and placebo arms,
respectively), and patient decision (3.8% and 3.5% in the canakinumab and placebo arms,
respectively; Appendix Table A1). Median duration of exposure to study treatment was similar
between arms (11.7 months [quartile 1 to quartile 3: 6.2-12.4] and 11.8 months [quartile 1 to
quartile 3: 6.2-12.4] in the canakinumab and placebo arms, respectively).

Patient Characteristics and Disposition
In total, 1,382 patients were randomly assigned to receive canakinumab (n = 693) or placebo (n =
689). Median time from random assignment to data cutoff (March 17, 2022) was 18.9 months
(range, 2.8-47.2). Baseline patient and disease characteristics were well balanced between arms
(Table 1).

CANOPY-A (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03447769) is a phase III, randomized, double-blind, multicenter study of canakinumab versus placebo for adult patients with stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-positives II-IIIB; American Joint Committee on Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control version 8), completely resected NSCLC who had received adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy. The primary end point was disease-free survival (DFS) and the key secondary end point was overall survival (OS).

Additional CANOPY-A study limitations include the long time
from diagnosis to random assignment, based, in part, on the need for chemotherapy in most
patients and radiation therapy in some; short time from random assignment to data cutoff; and
lack of pathologic confirmation of recurrent disease.
Although not formally tested, OS was numerically greater in the canakinumab arm compared with
the placebo arm. Only 100 deaths were reported, and the separation of the Kaplan-Meier curves
occurred after completion of therapy. Furthermore, LCSS rates were similar between the two
treatment arms, with relatively more patient deaths, not from lung cancer, in the placebo arm,
including cardiovascular disease–related fatalities.
Different factors may underpin the disparate results from CANTOS and CANOPY-A, such as the
differences in the populations recruited: patients had cardiovascular disease and myocardial
infarction versus patients with NSCLC, respectively. CRP level was higher in patients diagnosed with
lung cancer in CANTOS than in patients in CANOPY-A (6.0 mg/L v 1.8 mg/L, respectively).16
Nevertheless, the effect of canakinumab on CRP level in CANOPY-A was similar to that observed in
the CANTOS trial, with a significant CRP reduction of up to 41%,16 suggesting that canakinumab

At data cutoff, 337 (48.6%) and 349 (50.7%) patients had completed treatment in the canakinumab
and placebo arms, respectively. Treatment was discontinued before completion in 207 (29.9%) and
203 (29.5%) patients in the canakinumab and placebo arms, respectively (Appendix Table A1). The
main reasons for discontinuation were progressive disease (18.9% and 19.2% in the canakinumab
and placebo arms, respectively), AEs (4.5% and 4.2% in the canakinumab and placebo arms,
respectively), and patient decision (3.8% and 3.5% in the canakinumab and placebo arms,
respectively; Appendix Table A1). Median duration of exposure to study treatment was similar
between arms (11.7 months [quartile 1 to quartile 3: 6.2-12.4] and 11.8 months [quartile 1 to
quartile 3: 6.2-12.4] in the canakinumab and placebo arms, respectively).

In total, 1,382 patients were randomized to 200 mg canakinumab (n = 693) or placebo (n = 689) once every 3 weeks for 18 cycles. Grade ≥3 adverse events (AEs) were reported in 20.8% and 19.6% of patients receiving canakinumab and placebo, respectively; AEs led to discontinuation in 4.3% and 4.1% of patients in these groups, respectively. This study did not meet its primary end point. Median DFS was 35.0 months (canakinumab arm) and 29.7 months (placebo arm; hazard ratio, 0.94; 95% CI, 0.78 to 1.14; one-sided P = .258). DFS subgroup analyses did not show any meaningful differences between arms. As expected, because of canakinumab-driven IL-1β pathway inhibition, C-reactive protein and IL-6 levels decreased in the canakinumab arm versus placebo arm, but had no correlation with differential clinical outcomes. OS was not formally tested as DFS was not statistically significant.

significant. Other secondary end points included DFS and OS by biomarker subgroups, lung
cancer–specific survival (LCSS), safety, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity. Details of LCSS
methods and a supplementary analysis to estimate the treatment effects on the basis of DFS had
the COVID-19 pandemic not occurred are provided in Appendix 1 (online only).
Patient Population
Adult patients with completely resected, AJCC/UICC version 8 stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-
positive disease) NSCLC were recruited. Patients had received and recovered from at least two
cycles of cisplatin-based therapy within 60 days of surgery (except for patients with stage IIA
disease with no nodal involvement, unless recommended by the treating physician) and radiation
therapy if indicated per local practice. Eligible patients had adequate organ function and an
Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 0 or 1. Patients were eligible
regardless of driver mutation status. Patients with unresectable or metastatic disease, malignant
disease other than NSCLC, or lung interstitial disease grade ≥2, and those who had received
neoadjuvant therapy, were ineligible.
Assessments
Imaging (computed tomography [CT] and/or magnetic resonance imaging) was performed at
screening/baseline, once every 12 weeks during year 1, once every 26 weeks during years 2 and 3,
and annually during years 4 and 5. First disease recurrence was detected by CT scan and a biopsy
was performed in cases of nonconclusive radiologic evidence. Patients who discontinued treatment
were followed up once every 12 weeks for survival until the final OS analysis or until death, loss to
follow-up, or consent withdrawal for survival follow-up.
Safety was monitored. All adverse events (AEs) were reported for at least 130 days after the last
administration of canakinumab and graded according to the Common Terminology Criteria for AEs
version 5.
Biomarker analyses were conducted using tumor tissue and blood samples. Collection of archival,
resected tumor samples during screening was mandatory. Additional tumor samples (core,
excisional, and incisional biopsies) were collected at the time of disease recurrence. Correlative
analyses reported focus on the baseline tissue collection time point only, because of availability
from most patients, and depended on testing priority, tissue quality, and local regulations.
Collection of blood samples for circulating biomarker analyses (eg, IL-6 and CRP) was mandatory
at baseline, on treatment, at post-treatment follow-up, and at disease progression. Detailed
biomarker assessments are described in Appendix 1.
Statistical Analysis
The full analysis set comprised all patients assigned to study treatment by random assignment.
DFS of canakinumab versus placebo arms was analyzed using a stratified log-rank test at an overall
one-sided 2.5% significance level. This study had a two-look (Lan-DeMets) group sequential design
with an O'Brien-Fleming type α-spending function. A user-defined γ function (with γ = –3) was

CANOPY-A (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03447769) is a phase III, randomized, double-blind, multicenter study of canakinumab versus placebo for adult patients with stage II-IIIA or IIIB (T >5 cm, N2-positives II-IIIB; American Joint Committee on Cancer/Union for International Cancer Control version 8), completely resected NSCLC who had received adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy. The primary end point was disease-free survival (DFS) and the key secondary end point was overall survival (OS).

used as a β-spending function to define a nonbinding futility rule at the interim analysis of DFS.
The DFS distribution was estimated using the Kaplan-Meier method, and the sample size
calculation was based on the primary variable DFS. To ensure 90% power to detect a hazard ratio
(HR) of 0.716, a total of 392 DFS events needed to be observed. Further details of the sample size
and interim analysis are available in Appendix 1.
The secondary end point, OS, would be tested if DFS was statistically significant. LCSS distribution
was estimated using the Kaplan-Meier method; HRs and 95% CIs were calculated using a stratified
Cox model.
Ethical Oversight
This study (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03447769) was conducted in accordance with the
International Council for Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of
Pharmaceuticals for Human Use Harmonized Tripartite Guidelines for Good Clinical Practice, with
applicable local regulations, including the European Directive 2001/20/EC and US Code of Federal
Regulations Title 21, and the Declaration of Helsinki. The Protocol (online only) and all
amendments were reviewed and approved by an institutional review board/independent ethics
committee/research ethics board before study start. All patients provided written informed
consent before study start.

Additional CANOPY-A study limitations include the long time
from diagnosis to random assignment, based, in part, on the need for chemotherapy in most
patients and radiation therapy in some; short time from random assignment to data cutoff; and
lack of pathologic confirmation of recurrent disease.

Safety
Regardless of causality, only two AEs of any grade were reported in ≥10% of patients (Appendix
Table A2): both were less frequent in the canakinumab arm compared with the placebo arm (cough
[11.4% v 14.8%] and arthralgia [10.0% v 12.0%]). Grade 3/4 AEs were experienced by 139 (20.1%)
and 123 (17.9%) patients in the canakinumab and placebo arms, respectively, and grade 5 AEs
were experienced by five (0.7%) and 12 (1.7%) patients in these groups, respectively. Rates of AEs,
serious AEs (SAEs), and fatal SAEs were similar between arms (Table 2).
Treatment-related AEs (TRAEs) occurred in 38.9% and 31.5% of patients in the canakinumab and
placebo arms, respectively, of which 6.4% and 2.9% were grade 3/4. Only in the placebo arm were
grade 5 TRAEs (0.3%) reported (Table 2).
Infections and neutropenia are expected AEs associated with canakinumab. All infections (reported
by Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities version 25 system organ class) were reported with
comparable incidences between treatment arms, with grade 3/4 events reported in 3.3% and 2.8%,
and grade 5 events in 0.4% and 0.6%, of patients in the canakinumab and placebo arms,
respectively (Appendix Table A3). Neutropenia incidence was higher in the canakinumab arm
compared with the placebo arm (grade 3/4: 1.3% and 0.3%, respectively; no grade 5), as was
decreased neutrophil count (grade 3/4: 1.7% and 0.3%, respectively; no grade 5; Appendix Table
A2).
AEs leading to dose interruption occurred in 13.4% and 11.0% of patients in the canakinumab and
placebo arms, respectively; 5.9% and 3.3%, respectively, were grade 3/4, and no grade 5 AEs
leading to dose interruption were reported (Table 2). Any-grade TRAEs leading to discontinuation

In light of the COVID-19 pandemic and its potential impacts on treatment effects, a supplementary
analysis censored patients who died or discontinued treatment due to COVID-19, or who started
medication for suspected/confirmed COVID-19. On the basis of this analysis, there was no
treatment effect (HR, 0.96; 95% CI, 0.79 to 1.16).
The key secondary end point, OS, was not formally tested as DFS was not statistically significant
(OS HR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.48 to 1.08; Fig 4A). Only 100 events were reported: 44 (6.3%) in the

random assignment to the date of death due to lung cancer) was similar between arms (HR, 0.90;
95% CI, 0.53 to 1.52; Fig 4B). Overall, 60 LCSS events were observed, including 30 events in the
canakinumab arm and 30 events in the placebo arm. Fourteen patients on canakinumab and 26
patients on placebo died due to other reasons and were censored for the LCSS analysis.
